title: Codes, Lower Bounds, and Phase Transitions in the Symmetric Rendezvous Problem
abstract:
In the rendezvous problem, two parties with different labelings of the vertices of a complete graph are trying to meet at some vertex at the same time. It is well-known that if the parties have predetermined roles, then the strategy where one of them waits at one vertex, while the other visits all $n$ vertices in random order is optimal, taking at most $n$ steps and averaging about $n/2$. Anderson and Weber considered the symmetric rendezvous problem, where both parties must use the same randomized strategy. They analyzed strategies where the parties repeatedly play the optimal asymmetric strategy, determining their role independently each time by a biased coin-flip. By tuning the bias, Anderson and Weber achieved an expected meeting time of about $0.829 n$, which they conjectured to be asymptotically optimal.
  We change perspective slightly: instead of minimizing the expected meeting time, we seek to maximize the probability of meeting within a specified time $T$. The Anderson-Weber strategy, which fails with constant probability when $T= Θ(n)$, is not asymptotically optimal for large $T$ in this setting. Specifically, we exhibit a symmetric strategy that succeeds with probability $1-o(1)$ in $T=4n$ steps. This is tight: for any $α< 4$, any symmetric strategy with $T = αn$ fails with constant probability. Our strategy uses a new combinatorial object that we dub a "rendezvous code," which may be of independent interest.
  When $T \le n$, we show that the probability of meeting within $T$ steps is indeed asymptotically maximized by the Anderson-Weber strategy. Our results imply new lower bounds, showing that the best symmetric strategy takes at least $0.638 n$ steps in expectation. We also present some partial results for the symmetric rendezvous problem on other vertex-transitive graphs.
extracted_text:
arXiv:1609.01582v1  [math.CO]  6 Sep 2016
Codes, Lower Bounds, and Phase Transitions in the
Symmetric Rendezvous Problem
Varsha Dani∗
Thomas P. Hayes†
Cristopher Moore‡
Alexander Russell§
Abstract
In the rendezvous problem, two parties with diﬀerent labelings of the vertices of a complete graph
are trying to meet at some vertex at the same time. It is well-known that if the parties have prede-
termined roles, then the strategy where one of them waits at one vertex, while the other visits all n
vertices in random order is optimal, taking at most n steps and averaging about n/2. Anderson and We-
ber [3] considered the symmetric rendezvous problem, where both parties must use the same randomized
strategy. They analyzed strategies where the parties repeatedly play the optimal asymmetric strategy,
determining their role independently each time by a biased coin-ﬂip. By tuning the bias, Anderson and
Weber achieved an expected meeting time of about 0.829n, which they conjectured to be asymptotically
optimal.
We change perspective slightly: instead of minimizing the expected meeting time, we seek to maximize
the probability of meeting within a speciﬁed time T . The Anderson-Weber strategy, which fails with
constant probability when T = Θ(n), is not asymptotically optimal for large T in this setting. Speciﬁcally,
we exhibit a symmetric strategy that succeeds with probability 1 −o(1) in T = 4n steps. This is tight:
for any α < 4, any symmetric strategy with T = αn fails with constant probability. Our strategy uses a
new combinatorial object that we dub a “rendezvous code,” which may be of independent interest.
When T ≤n, we show that the probability of meeting within T steps is indeed asymptotically
maximized by the Anderson-Weber strategy. Our results imply new lower bounds, showing that the best
symmetric strategy takes at least 0.638n steps in expectation. We also present some partial results for
the symmetric rendezvous problem on other vertex-transitive graphs.
∗Department of Computer Science, University of New Mexico. varsha@cs.unm.edu
†Department of Computer Science, University of New Mexico. hayes@cs.unm.edu
‡Santa Fe Institute. moore@santafe.edu
§Computer Science and Engineering, University of Connecticut. acr@cse.uconn.edu


1
Introduction
The rendezvous problem is a cooperative game ﬁrst introduced by Alpern in 1976, who also called it the
“Mozart Caf´e problem” [1]:
Two friends agree to meet for lunch at the Mozart Caf´e in Vienna on the 1st of January 2000.
However, on arriving at Vienna airport, they are told there are three (or n) caf´es with that name,
no two close enough to visit in the same day. So, every day each can go to one of them, hoping
to ﬁnd his friend there. This is a player-symmetric problem, so what is the best randomized
strategy for both to adopt?
While the two players can each label the caf´es 1, . . . , n, they have no way to do this in common. Thus their
labelings diﬀer by a permutation π chosen uniformly at random from all n! permutations. How can they
ensure a rendezvous as soon as possible?
One approach is for one player to the same location every day, while the other visits them all in arbitrary
order—a strategy known as “Wait For Mommy” [2], since (in an analogous problem) children are often told
that if they can’t ﬁnd their parent, they should stay still while their parent looks for them. In this case, the
players will meet with certainty within n steps, and the expected time to meet is (n + 1)/2. It is easy to see
that this is optimal; we review here a proof from Anderson and Weber [3]. Recall the following lemma:
Lemma 1. For any non-negative integer-valued random variable τ,
E[τ] =
X
t≥0
P[τ > t] .
(1)
Let xt and yt denote the (private) labels chosen by the respective players at step t, and let τ be the rendezvous
time, that is, τ = min{t : xt = π(yt)}. Since π is uniformly random, the probability that xt = π(yt) for any
given t is 1/n. Therefore, the union bound tells us that P[τ ≤t] ≤t/n, and so
P[τ > t] ≥(1 −t/n)+ ,
where x+ denotes max(x, 0). Thus Lemma 1 gives
E[τ] ≥

2
.
(2)
The Wait For Mommy strategy matches this general lower bound, and is therefore optimal.
However, the Wait For Mommy strategy is an asymmetric strategy: it assumes that the players have
agreed on their respective roles in advance. In the symmetric rendezvous problem, they must independently
follow the same randomized strategy. This might occur, for instance, if two players are trying to ﬁnd a
shared radio frequency on which to communicate (see, e.g., [8]) without each knowing the other’s identity in
advance.
One simple symmetric strategy is for each player to go to a uniformly random location at each step: then
the expected time to meet is n, and the probability that they meet in the ﬁrst T steps is 1 −(1 −1/n)T ≈
1 −e−T/n. Another strategy is for each player to visit the n locations in order according to their labeling.
This succeeds in the ﬁrst n steps as long as the permutation π relating their labelings is not a derangement,
i.e., if it has at least one ﬁxed point π(x) = x. A classic exercise in combinatorics shows, using the inclusion-
exclusion formula, that the number of derangements is
n!

1 −1 + 1
2 −1
6 + · · · + (−1)n
n!

≈n!
e .
Since π is chosen uniformly at random from among all n! permutations, the probability that they meet in
the ﬁrst T = n steps is again roughly 1 −1/e.
1


We can also think of the rendezvous problem as a cooperative two-player game called Reduce The
Permanent. There is an n × n matrix M, whose entries are initially all 1s. At each step, one player chooses
a row x, the other chooses a column y, and they set Mxy = 0, corresponding to visiting locations x and y
according to their respective labelings. Recall that the permanent is
Perm M =
X
π
Y
y
Mπ(y),y ,
where the sum is over all n! permutations. Thus, at a given point in the game, the permanent of M is the
number of permutations π such that Mπ(y),y = 1 for all y. This is the number of permutations π such that,
if the players’ labelings diﬀer by π, they have not yet met; that is, they have not set any of the entries Mxy
where x = π(y) to zero. Therefore, the probability they have not yet met is
1
n! PermM .
In this setting, Wait For Mommy corresponds to keeping (say) x constant and progressively eliminating a
row of the matrix, setting Mxy = 0 for T distinct values of y. This reduces Perm M to zero after T = n
steps. On the other hand, if both players wander, then after T = n steps they eliminate the transversal
of the matrix corresponding to π; equivalently, after permuting the rows of the matrix, they eliminate the
diagonal. Then PermM is the number of derangements of n items, giving (1/n!) PermM ≈1/e. In the
symmetric setting, the sequences x1, . . . , xt, . . . and y1, . . . , yt, . . . must be chosen independently from the
same distribution. The challenge is then, in expectation, how quickly Perm Mt can be reduced as a function
of t, where Mt denotes the all-ones matrix modiﬁed so that all the (xi, yi) entries are zeroes, for 1 ≤i ≤t.
Anderson and Weber [3] considered symmetric strategies where, at the beginning of each block of n
moves, each player ﬂips a biased coin, and either waits or wanders for n steps `a la Wait For Mommy. Let us
add a distinguished label 0 for a “frequent” location which a player might visit repeatedly. With probability
θ each player waits for n steps at their location 0, and with probability 1 −θ they visit all n locations,
ordered by their labeling. With probability θ2 they both wait; then they only meet if π(0) = 0, which occurs
with probability 1/n. With probability (1 −θ)2 they both wander; then they meet if π has a ﬁxed point,
i.e., if it is not a derangement. Finally, with probability 2θ(1 −θ) they carry out the Wait For Mommy
strategy and meet with certainty. If they fail to meet, they each update their labeling with a uniformly
random permutation—in eﬀect, choosing a new uniformly random π—and try again for another block of n
steps. By optimizing over θ, Anderson and Weber showed that by setting θ = 0.249 . . . it is possible to meet
in expected time (0.829 . . .)n. They conjectured that this is asymptotically optimal, i.e., that the constant
0.829 . . . cannot be improved.
In the non-asymptotic regime, Weber [10, 9] has recently proved that the Anderson-Weber strategy is
optimal when n = 3, but not when n = 4.
Our contributions.
Rather than minimizing the expected time to meet, we focus on the time T it takes
to meet with high probability. The Anderson-Weber strategy fails with probability Ω(1) for any constant
number of rounds, i.e., for every ﬁnite α, we have P(τ > αn) = Ω(1). It is tempting to think that this is true
for every symmetric strategy. Surprisingly, this is false! Indeed, we show that P(τ > αn) can be made to tend
to zero as n →∞if and only if α ≥4. Speciﬁcally, we give a strategy for which P(τ > 4n) = O(1/ log n),
using a new combinatorial object we call a “rendezvous code.”
On the other hand, we show that if our goal is to maximize the probability of meeting within the ﬁrst T
steps, the Anderson-Weber strategy is indeed asymptotically optimal in the case T ≤n, in the sense that it
achieves the greatest possible success probability, up to an additive o(1) error. Note that the optimal bias
θ depends on T . We also show that any symmetric strategy requires, in expectation, at least T = 0.638n
steps. Our results suggest that for n < T < 4n, the optimal strategy may be an interesting interpolation
between the Anderson-Weber strategy and our rendezvous codes.
Finally, we consider the symmetric rendezvous problem on graphs G other than the complete graph [2].
Now the players are only allowed to move along G’s edges at each step; on the other hand, the permutation π
2


is limited to the automorphism group Aut(G). To keep the problem interesting, we focus on vertex-transitive
graphs, so that there is no distinguished vertex where they can arrange to meet. We show that if G is the
cycle, where their labelings diﬀer by a random translation and (with probability 1/2) a ﬂip between clockwise
and counterclockwise, the probability of failure is Ω(1) for any T = O(n). On the other hand, we show that
on a cycle of prime length they can meet with high probability in T = n steps if we decorate the cycle
with some additional edges. We also show how our rendezvous codes can be adapted to arbitrary graphs of
diameter o(n), giving a symmetric strategy which guarantees a failure rate of o(1) when T ≥8n. We close
with open questions and directions for further work.
2
Notation and Preliminaries
It will be helpful to adopt formal deﬁnitions for some terminology that will be used throughout the paper.
First, we deﬁne the space from which each player must make a selection; we call its elements “walk schedules.”
Deﬁnition 1. A walk schedule is a (ﬁnite or countably inﬁnite) sequence of labels from {0, 1, . . ., n}. We
view label 0 as an alternative notation for label n.
This is for notational convenience, as shall become
apparent later. A player, having selected a walk schedule x = (x1, x2, . . . ) in the rendezvous game, visits, at
each step t, the vertex labeled xt under her own private vertex labeling.
In section 4, we will generalize the notion of walk schedule to graphs other than the complete graph.
We think of a game in which two players each choose a walk schedule independently of each other. A
third party, known as the Adversary, chooses a permutation π of the n + 1 vertices, independently and
without any knowledge of the walk schedules.
Deﬁnition 2. The rendezvous time, τ is deﬁned as τ = min{t : xt = π(yt)}.
The two players collaborate to try to minimize τ, while the Adversary tries to maximize τ. Thinking of the
two players as a single team, we have the following notions of what their strategies can be.
Deﬁnition 3. A (mixed) strategy is a distribution over pairs of walk schedules. A strategy is symmetric
if it is of the form D × D; that is, each player independently samples their walk schedule from the same
distribution D.
As for the Adversary, her optimal strategy is to choose a uniformly random permutation. To see this, observe
that either or both of the players can randomly permute the labels used in their own walk schedule, which
has the same eﬀect. For this reason, we will henceforth assume π is chosen uniformly at random, and forget
about the Adversary.
Our next deﬁnition allows us to analyze arbitrary strategies in a “Wait For Mommy”-like way, classifying
steps as “waiting” if a player visits a small number of locations many times, and “wandering” if she visits a
large number of places a few times each.
Deﬁnition 4. Let x be a walk schedule. If a label ℓappears more than n2/3 times in x, we call ℓfrequent
for x; otherwise we call ℓrare. We refer to t as a waiting step for x if xt is a frequent label, and a wandering
step if xt is rare; more colloquially, we say that “x waits” or “x wanders” on step t.
At this point, two comments should be made. First, when we talk about symmetric strategies, the walk
schedules should always be of inﬁnite length. This is because, for any ﬁnite T , there is a nonzero chance
that both players will choose the same label sequence for the ﬁrst T steps, in which case there is at least a
1/e conditional probability that τ > T .
On the other hand, we shall be considering probabilities of the form P(τ ≥αn), for which we may as well
assume the walk sequences are truncated to length αn. It is in the latter context that we will be applying
the terminology from Deﬁnition 4. Note that, in this case, counting shows that there can be at most αn1/3
rare labels per walk sequence.
Deﬁnition 5. We say that a strategy is of Anderson-Weber type, if for each j = 0, 1, 2, . . ., with probability
one, the walk sequence in the block of steps {jn + 1, jn + 2, . . . , (j + 1)n} is either 0n or a permutation of
1, . . . , n.
3


3
The (Almost) Complete Story on the Complete Graph
In this section we present our main results on the symmetric rendezvous problem for the complete graph.
Speciﬁcally, we show that using “rendezvous codes” rather than Anderson-Weber strategies, it is possible
to rendezvous with high probability in 4n steps; but that for T = αn with α < 4, any symmetric strategy
fails with probability Ω(1). This result came as a big surprise to us: we were initially convinced that the
optimal strategy was of Anderson-Weber type, or at least that it consists of independent rounds of length
n, but clearly for some T < 4n this ceases to be the case. On the other hand, we also show that for T ≤n,
the probability of success is asymptotically maximized by Anderson-Weber strategies.
3.1
A bound based on waiting and wandering
We begin with an intuitive yet technical lemma which is crucial to the rest of the paper. In addition to
motivating our deﬁnition of rendezvous codes, presented in Section 3.2, it also underlies all of our lower
bounds.
Lemma 2. Let x and y be two walk schedules of length T = O(n). Let a, b, c, d be as follows:
• a is the number of steps when both x and y wait,
• b is the number of steps when x waits and y wanders,
• c is the number of steps when x wanders and y waits, and
• d is the number of steps when both x and y wander.
Note that a + b + c + d = T . Then the probability that two players playing according to schedules x and y
fail to rendezvous by time T is bounded below by
P[τ > T ] ≥(1 −b/n)+(1 −c/n)+e−d/n −o(1) .
(3)
Before we present the proof, we brieﬂy describe some intuition behind it. On steps when both players
wait, there is a o(1) chance of meeting because each player has O(n1/3) frequent labels. On steps when one
player waits and the other wanders, they are, at best, executing the Wait For Mommy strategy. This gives
rise to the terms (1 −b/n)+ and (1 −c/n)+. On steps when both players wander, they meet on each step
with probability 1/n. If these events were independent, then the probability of failing to meet would be
(1 −1/n)d ≈e−d/n. If these four “phases” were run independently, we would obtain the right-hand side of
(3). We remark that, from the above observations, it is not hard to see that the right-hand side of (3) is
tight up to the additive o(1) term for all values of a, b, c, d.
Our proof will rely on the following result about the permanent of a zero-one matrix with relatively few
zeroes in every row and column.
Lemma 3. Let M be an m × m matrix of zeroes and ones, and let Z ⊂[m] × [m] be the index set of its
zeroes. Suppose M has no more than κ zeroes in any row or column, and that κ = o(m). Then, for a
randomly chosen permutation π,
P(∀(x, y) ∈Z, π(y) ̸= x) = Perm(M)

3.2
Rendezvous codes
Lemma 2 tells us that, in order to guarantee a rendezvous by time T with high probability, we need to ensure
that at least one of b or c is n −o(n) with high probability. In other words, our symmetric strategy must
have Wait For Mommy hidden inside it with high probability. This motivates the following deﬁnition.
Deﬁnition 6. A rendezvous code is a collection R of walk schedules such that, for every two distinct
schedules x, y ∈R, there is a set of n steps in which x has every label exactly once, while y repeats the
same label n times.
If R is a rendezvous code of length T and size s, then the symmetric strategy in which both players
independently sample their walk schedule from R will ensure a meeting within T steps whenever their
schedules are distinct, which occurs with probability at least 1−1/s. In fact, it ensures at least two meetings
with probability at least 1 −1/s −1/n, since if x and y are distinct, each player will wait while the other
6


one wanders through all n locations. (The 1/n term corresponds to the possibility that the players’ repeated
labels correspond to the same vertex.)
It is not obvious that rendezvous codes exist of length O(n) and size ω(1). In this section, we present
rendezvous codes of length T = 4n and size Ω(log n), thus ensuring a rendezvous in at most 4n steps with
probability 1 −O(1/ log n). As a result, Anderson-Weber-type strategies do not maximize the probability of
success for T ≥4n. As we will show below, the constant 4 is tight: the shortest possible rendezvous codes
of size ω(1), and more generally, the shortest possible symmetric strategies that succeed with probability
1 −o(1), have length 4n −o(n).
Our construction of rendezvous codes of length O(n) and size Ω(log n) is as follows. There will be a
unique frequent label, 0, that occurs T/2 = 2n times in every walk schedule. The remaining labels, 1, . . . , n,
are rare, with each occuring exactly twice in every walk schedule. Let n = 2d and T = 4n = 2d+2, and
consider the (d + 2) × T binary matrix M whose t’th column is the binary representation of t for 0 ≤t < T .
Speciﬁcally, let Mi,t be the ith most signiﬁcant bit of the binary representation of t. For n = 4, in which
case d = 2 and T = 16, this matrix is
M =

We will use M to construct another matrix M ′ such that each row of M ′ corresponds to a walk schedule.

The rows of M ′ constitute a rendezvous code of length T and size s = d + 2. To see this, let x and
y be the ith and jth row respectively, where i ̸= j. There are T/4 = n columns t where Mi,t = 1 and
Mj,t = 0. After removing the ith row, the columns t where Mi,t = 1 range over all 2d+1 binary sequences,
so the corresponding labels xt = M ′
i,t range over all ℓ∈[n]. Moreover, since each pair of columns with the
same label is complementary, exactly one of them occurs when yt = Mj,t = 0. Thus for each 1 ≤ℓ≤n there
is a t where xt = M ′
i,t = ℓand yt = M ′
j,t = 0, so this set of T/4 = n columns satisﬁes the deﬁnition of the
rendezvous code.
Now suppose the two players independently choose a walk schedule uniformly from the rows of M ′, and
that these rows are distinct. Using 0 to denote their frequent locations, in the unlikely event that π(0) = 0
they meet on the very ﬁrst step. Otherwise, they meet at the time t such that yt = 0 and xt = π(0), and
again (if they choose to continue) at the t where xt = 0 and yt = π−1(0). Thus this strategy only fails in
the event that i = j and π is a derangement, and this occurs with probability O(1/s) = O(1/ log n).
There is an easy generalization of this construction to the case where n is not a power of 2. Suppose
n ≤n′ = k2d. We construct the (d + 2) × 2d+2 matrix M as before, but now repeat each row k times. In the
ﬁrst copy, we set the nonzero entries of M ′ so that they range from 1 to 2d as before; in the second copy, we
add 2d to these entries so that they range from 2d + 1 to 2 · 2d, and so on. If we replace entries of M ′ greater
than n with 0, this produces a rendezvous code of size s = d + 2 and length 2d+2k = 4n′. By setting, say,
d = (1/2)⌈log2 n⌉and k = ⌊(n −1)/2d⌋, we obtain s = Ω(log n) and n′ = n + o(n), so T = 4n + o(n).
Thus we have proved
7


Theorem 1. For any n, there is a symmetric rendezvous strategy on the complete graph Kn for which
P(τ ≤4n) ≥1 −O(1/ log n).
3.3
More rendezvous codes
It is clear from the deﬁnition of rendezvous codes that we can form them by concatenation. That is, given
two rendezvous codes R1, R2, the code R1R2 = {r1r2 : r1 ∈R1, r2 ∈R2} is a rendezvous code as well.
Concatenating the rendezvous code given in the previous section with itself c times gives a rendezvous code
of length 4cn and size O(logc n).
Of course, this simply corresponds to performing our previous strategy c times independently, reducing
the probability of failure to O(1/ logc n). In analogy with error-correcting codes, we suspect that there are
much better constructions. One interesting question is whether there is a rendezvous code of length O(n)
and polynomial size, allowing the players to meet with probability 1 −1/poly(n) in linear time.
In an eﬀort to explore possible constructions of rendezvous codes a little further, we can construct M
and M ′ using some base other than binary. As we will see, this gives rendezvous codes that achieve any
rational fraction p of time spent waiting versus wandering, and also somewhat reduces the expected time to
meet. In this section, which is not essential to the rest of the paper, we sketch out the main ideas of this
construction; details are left to the interested reader.
Fix constants A and B with 0 < A < B. Set n = (B −A)Bk−1, and let
T = Bk+1 =

Let M be the (k + 1) × T matrix whose columns are the base-B representations of the numbers 0 ≤t < T .
As in the binary case, we will use M to construct a matrix M ′ whose rows are walk schedules. However, now
B −A of the possible entries of M will correspond to wandering. Speciﬁcally, an entry m ∈{1, . . ., B −A}
corresponds to wandering among the Bk−1 locations (m −1)Bk−1 + 1, (m −1)Bk−1 + 2, . . . , mBk−1. The
remaining A possible entries of M correspond to waiting at the frequent location, which we again denote 0;
thus if Mi,t /∈{1, . . . , B −A} we set M ′
i,t = 0. The fraction of time spent waiting is then p = A/B.
It remains to deﬁne a location M ′
i,t in the case Mi,t = m ∈{1, . . . , B −A}. Fix a row i, and consider

B columns each, where t and t′ are equivalent if, for some z ∈{0, . . . , B−1}, we have Mj,t ≡Mj,t′ +z mod B
for all j ̸= i. Within each equivalence class, there is a unique t′ such that 0 ≤wt′ < Bk−1. Set ℓt = wt′ + 1
for all t ≡t′, and ﬁnally set M ′
i,t = (m −1)Bk−1 + ℓt.
To see this is a rendezvous code, let 1 ≤x ≤n, and write x = (m −1)Bk−1 + ℓ. Let i and j be distinct,

A steps t where Mj,t /∈{1, . . ., B −A}, so that M ′
j,t = 0. On any of these steps, we have xt = x and yt = 0.
Varying the constants A, B gives a family of codes of length T = O(n) and size s = k + 1 = Ω(log n).
While the length T is minimized by the binary case B = 2, it turns out that we can decrease the expected
rendezvous time by taking B > 2, as we will now show.
It is convenient for our analysis to further modify the code by permuting its T columns randomly. In
that case, we obtain bounds that are similar to Lemma 2, but which are essentially tight.

Proof. Let ε > 0 be a constant which we will determine below. By the pigeonhole principle, there must exist
some w such that with probability at least ε, a walk schedule sampled from D waits for between wT and

We will condition on this event happening.
By adding the correct number of waiting steps at the end of each of these walk schedules, we can deﬁne a

3.5
Anderson-Weber strategies are asymptotically optimal for T ≤n
In this section, we show that strategies of Anderson-Weber type asymptotically maximize the probability
of meeting within a ﬁxed deadline T ≤n, up to an additive o(1) error. Of course, with such a short time
horizon, there is only time for the ﬁrst of the wait-or-wander coin ﬂips, so it is a very special case. On the
other hand, these early steps are the biggest contributors to the sum in (1), so their importance should not
be underestimated.
Theorem 3. For each T ≤n, P[τ ≥T ] is asymptotically minimized, up to an additive o(1) error, over all
symmetric strategies, by some strategy of Anderson-Weber type.
Our proof will use some tools from the minimization of submodular functions on lattices.
Deﬁnition 7. Let S be a ﬁnite set of size n, and let f : 2S →R be a real-valued function on subsets of S.
We say f is submodular if, for every X, Y ⊆S, we have
f(X ∩Y ) + f(X ∪Y ) ≤f(X) + f(Y ) .
We begin by recalling some basic tools for constructing submodular functions. The ﬁrst of these is a
standard connection between submodularity and concavity1 (e.g. [6, Prop. 5.1]):
Proposition 1. Suppose S is a ﬁnite set, and f : 2S →R is deﬁned by f(Z) = g(|Z|) for some g :
{0, . . . , |S|} →R. Then f is submodular if and only if g is concave.
We will also use the following easy consequence of the distributive law:
Proposition 2. If f(Z) is submodular then, for any ﬁxed X, f(X ∩Y ) is a submodular function of Y .
Finally, we will use the following fact about products of submodular functions (e.g. [4, Ex. 3.32(b)]).
Proposition 3. Let g and h be non-negative, submodular functions on the same domain. Suppose g is
non-decreasing with respect to set inclusion, and h is non-increasing. Then their product gh is submodular.
Using these, we can prove that the function featured in Lemma 2 is submodular. More precisely,
Proposition 4. Let T ≤n and let X, Y ⊆[T ]. Let a = |X ∩Y |, b = |X ∩Y |, c = |X ∩Y |, and d = |X ∩Y |.
Denote
f(X, Y ) = (1 −b/n)(1 −c/n) e−d/n.
Then f is submodular in each of its arguments.
1A function f deﬁned on a discrete set S is concave if and only if it can be extended to a concave function on the convex
hull of S.
11


We remark that the assumption T ≤n cannot be eliminated. Also note that, when x, y are walk schedules of
length T ≤n and X, Y are the respective sets of wandering steps, then a, b, c, d are the same as in Lemma 2
and the conclusion of Lemma 2 is that
P(τ > T ) ≥f(X, Y ) −o(1) .
Proof of Proposition 4. Since f is symmetric, it suﬃces to prove that, for each ﬁxed X, f(X, Y ) is submod-

Clearly g(Z) is a concave, non-increasing function of |Z|. Using a little calculus and taking into account the
fact that |Z| ≤|X|, we see that h(Z) is a concave, non-decreasing function of |Z|. By Proposition 1, both
are then submodular functions of Z, and Proposition 2 implies that g(X ∩Y ) and h(X ∩Y ) are submodular
functions of Y . Finally, g(X ∩Y ) is non-increasing, h(X ∩Y ) is non-decreasing, and both are non-negative,
so Proposition 3 implies that f(X, Y ) is submodular in Y .
Deﬁnition 8. Let x ∈[0, 1]S. We say that a distribution φ over subsets of S has expectation x if, for all
s ∈S, PS′∼φ[s ∈S′] = x(s).
The Lov´asz extension of x is the distribution over 2S that samples λ ∈[0, 1] uniformly at random, and
outputs the subset {s ∈S : x(s) ≥λ}. Note that this distribution has expectation x. Moreover, its support
forms a chain S0 ⊆· · · ⊆Sk; for instance, if the x(s) are distinct and less than 1, we have k = n, S0 = ∅,
and Si = Si−1 ∪{si} where x(s1) < x(s2) < · · · < x(sn).
The Lov´asz extension plays an important role in the minimization of submodular functions because of
the following property (see, for instance, [5, Theorem 3.7]):
Proposition 5. Let f : 2S →R be submodular, and let x ∈[0, 1]S. Then, among all distributions whose
expectation is x, the Lov´asz extension of x minimizes the expected value of f.
This means that, for a ﬁxed expectation x ∈[0, 1]S, every submodular function is minimized by the same
distribution, whose support forms a chain.
Rather than directly applying Proposition 5, we will need the following “two-player” analog of it.
Proposition 6. Let S be a ﬁnite set, and let f : 2S × 2S →R be submodular in each of its arguments. Let
x ∈[0, 1]S, and let ψ be the Lov´asz extension of x. Then the distribution ψ × ψ minimizes the expectation of
f among all distributions φ × φ such that φ has expectation x.
Proof. The proof is a straightforward “uncrossing” argument along the lines of [5, Lemma 3.6]. Since S is
ﬁnite, the space of all probability distributions over 2S is compact. Hence, by the Extreme Value Theorem,
there exists a distribution φ that, among all distributions with expectation x, minimizes Eφ×φ(f), and,
subject to both these constraints, maximizes VarX∼φ(|X|). We will prove that this φ must, in fact, be the
Lov´asz extension of x.
Suppose X, Y ⊆S were two incomparable subsets in the support of φ. Then, taking δ = min{φ(X), φ(Y )},
we can deﬁne a modiﬁed distribution φ′ by decreasing φ(X) and φ(Y ) by δ, and increasing φ(X ∪Y ) and
φ(X ∩Y ) by δ; φ′ is otherwise equal to φ. This preserves the expectation x. Moreover, it preserves or
decreases the expectation of f, since
Eφ′×φ′[f] −Eφ×φ[f] = (Eφ′×φ[f] −Eφ×φ[f]) + (Eφ′×φ′[f] −Eφ′×φ[f])
= δ EZ∼φ[f(X ∪Y, Z) + f(X ∩Y, Z) −f(X, Z) −f(Y, Z)]
+ δ EZ∼φ′[f(Z, X ∪Y ) + f(Z, X ∩Y ) −f(Z, X) −f(Z, X)]
≤0 ,
12


where the last line follows since f is symmetric and submodular in both arguments. Finally, we observe that
VarZ∼φ′|Z| = VarZ∼φ|Z| + 2δ |X \ Y | |Y \ X| > VarZ∼φ|Z| .
But this contradicts our choice of φ. Hence our assumption that φ contained incomparable elements was
false, so the support of φ is a chain. Since the expectation of φ is x, it must be the Lov´asz extension of
x.
As a corollary, we will deduce that the expectation of a concave function of two variables is minimized
for two-point distributions:
Proposition 7. Let g(t1, t2) be concave in each of its arguments, and suppose that t1 and t2 are chosen
independently from a distribution P supported on {0, . . . , T }. Then, among all distributions P with a given
expectation µ = EP [t], the expectation EP ×P [g] is minimized by a distribution P supported only at the
extremes of the interval: P(T ) = µ/T , P(0) = 1 −µ/T , and P(t) = 0 for all other t.
Proof. Let S = [T ] and deﬁne a function f : 2S × 2S →R by f(X, Y ) = g(|X|, |Y |). By Proposition 1, f is
submodular in each of its arguments.
Let P be a distribution on {0, . . . , T } of mean µ. We deﬁne a distribution φ on 2S by coupling it with
P. For each 0 ≤t ≤T , whenever t is sampled from P, let φ produce a uniformly random subset of S of size
t. Note that our deﬁnition ensures that Eφ×φ(f) = EP ×P (g).
Deﬁne x ∈[0, 1]S by xi = µ/T for every 1 ≤i ≤T . Observe that x equals the expectation of φ. By
Proposition 6, f is minimized at ψ ×ψ, where ψ is the Lov´asz extension of x. However, the Lov´asz extension
of x is supported on just two points: ∅with probability 1 −µ/T , and [T ] with probability µ/T . It follows
that
EP ×P (g) = Eψ×ψ(f)
by inspection,
≤Eφ×φ(f)
by Proposition 6,
= EP ×P (g)
by the deﬁnition of φ,
which was to be proved.
Our next proposition narrows the optimal strategies down further to those that wait for some number of
steps and wander for the remaining steps:
Proposition 8. For each T ≤n, it is possible to deﬁne a probability distribution P(t) on [0, T ] such that
the following symmetric strategy maximizes the probability of a successful rendezvous, up to an additive o(1):
each player independently samples a value of t according to P(t), waits at location 0 on steps {1, 2, . . ., t}
and wanders through T −t distinct locations on steps {t + 1, . . . , T }.
Proof. Let T ≤n and let D×D be a symmetric strategy. As before, we consider walk sequences x, y sampled
from D × D, and let X, Y ⊆[T ] denote the corresponding set of wandering steps. Also as before, let a, b, c, d
be as deﬁned in Lemma 2, and let
f(X, Y ) =

the expectation of φ, and let ψ denote the Lov´asz extension of x. Then we have
P(τ > T ) ≥E(X,Y )∼φ×φ[f(X, Y )] −o(1)

Now, since the support of ψ is a chain, and we can permute the steps {1, . . . , T } without changing the
success probability, we can, without loss of generality, push all the waiting steps to the front. This means
that, under ψ, we can assume that the set, X, of wandering steps is always of the form {t + 1, . . . , T }.
Considering, for instance, (4), we see that, up to an additive o(1), it is optimal to choose all distinct
labels for the wandering steps, which completes the proof.
We are now ready to prove the main result of the section.
Proof of Theorem 3. Suppose D is an asymptotically optimal strategy of the type described in Proposition 8,
and let P be the distribution described in Proposition 8.
We condition on the waiting times t1, t2 sampled independently from P by the two players.
In the
notation of Lemma 2, we have b = (t1 −t2)+, c = (t2 −t1)+, and d = T −max(t1, t2). Thus the probability
of failing to meet is g(t1, t2) + o(1), where
g(t1, t2) =

1 −|t1 −t2|
n

e(max(t1,t2)−T )/n .
It is easily veriﬁed that g(t1, t2) is concave in each of t1 and t2 separately. To see this, ﬁrst note that g is
continuous: then, ﬁxing t1, the second derivative of g with respect to t2 is zero for t2 < t1 and negative for
t2 > t1. Thus g is concave as a function of t2, and by symmetry as a function of t1 as well.
Up to o(1), the probability of failure is the expectation of g(t1, t2) where t1, t2 are chosen independently
according to P. Since g is concave in both of its arguments, Proposition 7 shows that, for any ﬁxed µ = E[t],
the expectation of g is minimized when P is supported on {0, T }. That is, setting θ = µ/T , with probability
P(T ) = θ we wait for T steps, and with probability P(0) = 1 −θ we wander for T steps. Thus the strategy
is of Anderson-Weber type, completing the proof.
3.6
Bounding failure and expected time in symmetric strategies
We conclude our discussion of the complete graph by using the results of the previous section to bound the
probability of failure, and the expected time to meet, for any symmetric strategy.
First, now that we know the asymptotically optimal strategy for T ≤n is of Anderson-Weber type, what
is the optimal value of the waiting probability θ? Using Lemma 2, the probability of failure in the ﬁrst T ≤n
steps is
P[no rendezvous by time T] = θ2 + 2θ(1 −θ)(1 −T/n) + (1 −θ)2 e−T/n −o(1) .
(9)
For a given T , this is minimized by
θ = 1 −(1 −T/n) eT/n
1 −(1 −2T/n) eT/n .
(10)
In particular, for T = n this recovers the bias 1/(e + 1) = 0.269 . . . of the original Anderson-Weber strategy.
Plugging this θ back into (9) gives
P[τ > T ] = 1 −(1 −T/n)2 eT/n
1 −(1 −2T/n) eT/n −o(1) .
(11)
Thus, the right-hand side of (11) is a lower bound for the failure probability of any symmetric strategy.

This implies
Proposition 9. For every symmetric strategy, the expected time to the ﬁrst rendezvous is bounded by
E[τ] ≥0.6389n −o(n) .
This bound is not tight: for instance, we can get a slight further improvement by applying the bound of
Theorem 2 for values of T up to 4n. More importantly, our bound is derived by analyzing each summand
P(τ > T ) in the sum (1) separately, even though we know that for the optimal Anderson-Weber strategies,
these correspond to diﬀerent values of θ.
In particular, it is still possible that the expected time (0.829 . . .)n achieved by the Anderson-Weber
strategy is asymptotically optimal. Nevertheless, since the asymmetric Wait For Mommy strategy achieves
E[τ] = n/2 −o(n), Proposition 9 shows that the ﬁrst rendezvous takes Θ(n) longer in expectation when we
are restricted to symmetric strategies.
4
Rendezvous on Other Graphs
In this section we consider the rendezvous problem on general graphs G. Each player has a map of G with
vertices labeled 1, . . . , n, and their labelings diﬀer by a permutation π chosen uniformly at random from the
automorphism group Aut(G).
Unless G is the complete graph (or the empty graph) Aut(G) is a proper subgroup of the permutation
group on the n vertices. In particular, not all pairs of vertices can be mapped onto ea
